Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## DATASET CONDENSATION WITH GRADIENT MATCHING

## **Bo Zhao, Konda Reddy Mopuri, Hakan Bilen**

School of Informatics, The University of Edinburgh _{_ bo.zhao, kmopuri, hbilen _}_ @ed.ac.uk

## ABSTRACT

As the state-of-the-art machine learning methods in many fields rely on larger datasets, storing datasets and training models on them become significantly more expensive. This paper proposes a training set synthesis technique for _data-efficient_ learning, called _Dataset Condensation_ , that learns to condense large dataset into a small set of informative synthetic samples for training deep neural networks from scratch. We formulate this goal as a gradient matching problem between the gradients of deep neural network weights that are trained on the original and our synthetic data. We rigorously evaluate its performance in several computer vision benchmarks and demonstrate that it significantly outperforms the state-of-the-art methods[1] . Finally we explore the use of our method in continual learning and neural architecture search and report promising gains when limited memory and computations are available.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Large-scale datasets, comprising millions of samples, are becoming the norm to obtain state-ofthe-art machine learning models in multiple fields including computer vision, natural language processing and speech recognition. At such scales, even storing and preprocessing the data becomes burdensome, and training machine learning models on them demands for specialized equipment and infrastructure. An effective way to deal with large data is data selection – identifying the most representative training samples – that aims at improving _data efficiency_ of machine learning techniques. While classical data selection methods, also known as coreset construction (Agarwal et al., 2004; Har-Peled & Mazumdar, 2004; Feldman et al., 2013), focus on clustering problems, recent work can be found in continual learning (Rebuffi et al., 2017; Toneva et al., 2019; Castro et al., 2018; Aljundi et al., 2019) and active learning (Sener & Savarese, 2018) where there is typically a fixed budget in storing and labeling training samples respectively. These methods commonly first define a criterion for representativeness ( _e.g_ . in terms of compactness (Rebuffi et al., 2017; Castro et al., 2018), diversity (Sener & Savarese, 2018; Aljundi et al., 2019), forgetfulness (Toneva et al., 2019)), then select the representative samples based on the criterion, finally use the selected small set to train their model for a downstream task.

Unfortunately, these methods have two shortcomings: they typically rely on i) heuristics ( _e.g_ . picking cluster centers) that does not guarantee any optimal solution for the downstream task ( _e.g_ . image classification), ii) presence of representative samples, which is neither guaranteed. A recent method, Dataset Distillation (DD) (Wang et al., 2018) goes beyond these limitations by _learning_ a small set of informative images from large training data. In particular, the authors model the network parameters as a function of the synthetic training data and learn them by minimizing the training loss over the original training data w.r.t. synthetic data. Unlike in the coreset methods, the synthesized data are directly optimized for the downstream task and thus the success of the method does not rely on the presence of representative samples.

Inspired from DD (Wang et al., 2018), we focus on learning to _synthesize informative samples_ that are optimized to train neural networks for downstream tasks and not limited to individual samples in original dataset. Like DD, our goal is to obtain the highest generalization performance with a model trained on a small set of synthetic images, ideally comparable performance to that of a model trained on the original images (see Figure 1(a)). In particular, we investigate the following

questions. Is it possible to i) compress a large image classification dataset into a small synthetic set, ii) train an image classification model on the synthetic set that can be further used to classify real images, iii) learn a single set of synthetic images that can be used to train different neural network architectures? To this end, we propose a _Dataset Condensation_ method to learn a small set of “condensed” synthetic samples such that a deep neural network trained on them obtains not only similar performance but also a close solution to a network trained on the large training data in the network parameter space. We formulate this goal as a minimization problem between two sets of gradients of the network parameters that are computed for a training loss over a large fixed training set and a learnable condensed set (see Figure 1(b)). We show that our method enables effective learning of synthetic images and neural networks trained on them, outperforms (Wang et al., 2018) and coreset methods with a wide margin in multiple computer vision benchmarks. In addition, learning a compact set of synthetic samples also benefits other learning problems when there is a fixed budget on training images. We show that our method outperforms popular data selection methods by providing more informative training samples in continual learning. Finally, we explore a promising use case of our method in neural architecture search, and show that – once our condensed images are learned – they can be used to train numerous network architectures extremely efficiently.

Our method is related to knowledge distillation (KD) techniques (Hinton et al., 2015; Buciluˇa et al., 2006; Ba & Caruana, 2014; Romero et al., 2014) that transfer the knowledge in an ensemble of models to a single one. Unlike KD, we distill knowledge of a large training set into a small synthetic set. Our method is also related to Generative Adversarial Networks (Goodfellow et al., 2014a; Mirza & Osindero, 2014; Radford et al., 2015) and Variational AutoEncoders (Kingma & Welling, 2013) that synthesize high-fidelity samples by capturing the data distribution. In contrast, our goal is to generate informative samples for training deep neural networks rather than to produce “real-looking” samples. Finally our method is related to the methods that produce image patches by projecting the feature activations back to the input pixel space (Zeiler & Fergus, 2014), reconstruct the input image by matching the feature activations (Mahendran & Vedaldi, 2015), recover private training images for given training gradients (Zhu et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2020), synthesize features from semantic embeddings for zero-shot learning (Sariyildiz & Cinbis, 2019). Our goal is however to synthesize a set of condensed training images not to recover the original or missing training images.

In the remainder of this paper, we first review the problem of dataset condensation and introduce our method in section 2, present and analyze our results in several image recognition benchmarks in section 3.1, showcase applications in continual learning and network architecture search in section 3.2, and conclude the paper with remarks for future directions in section 4.

In practice, _**θ**[T]_ for different initializations can be trained first in an offline stage and then used as the target parameter vector in eq. (5). However, there are two potential issues by learning to regress _**θ**[T]_ as the target vector. First the distance between _**θ**[T]_ and intermediate values of _**θ**[S]_ can be too big in the parameter space with multiple local minima traps along the path and thus it can be too challenging to reach. Second opt-alg involves a limited number of optimization steps as a tradeoff between speed and accuracy which may not be sufficient to take enough steps for reaching the optimal solution. These problems are similar to those of (Wang et al., 2018), as they both involve parameterizing _**θ**[S]_ with _S_ and _**θ**_ 0.

**Discussion.** The synthetic data contains not only samples but also their labels ( _**s** , y_ ) that can be jointly learned by optimizing eq. (9) in theory. **Algorithm.** We depict the optimization details in Alg. 1. At the outer level, it contains a loop over random weight initializations, as we want to obtain condensed images that can later be used to train previously unseen models. Once _**θ**_ is randomly initialized, we use _φ_ _**θ**_ to first compute the loss over both the training samples ( _L[T]_ ), synthetic samples ( _L[S]_ ) and their gradients w.r.t. _**θ**_ , then optimize the synthetic samples _S_ to match these gradients _∇_ _**θ** L[S]_ to _∇_ _**θ** L[T]_ by applying _ςS_ gradient descent steps with learning rate _ηS_ . We use the stochastic gradient descent optimization for both opt-alg _**θ**_ and opt-alg _S_ . Next we train _**θ**_ on the updated synthetic images by minimizing the loss _L[S]_ with learning rate _η_ _**θ**_ for _ς_ _**θ**_ steps. Note that we sample each real and synthetic batch pair from _T_ and _S_ containing samples from a single class and the synthetic data for each class are separately (or parallelly) updated at each iteration ( _t_ ) for the following reasons: i) this reduces memory use at train time, ii) imitating the mean gradients w.r.t. the data from single class is easier compared to those of multiple classes. This does not bring any extra computational cost.

In contrast to (Lopez-Paz et al., 2017; Aljundi et al., 2019; Zhu et al., 2019) that ignore the layer-wise structure by flattening tensors over all layers to one vector and then computing the distance between two vectors, we group them for each output node. We found that this is a better distance for gradient matching (see the supplementary) and enables using a single learning rate across all layers.

## 3 EXPERIMENTS

## 3.1 DATASET CONDENSATION

First we evaluate classification performance with the condensed images on four standard benchmark datasets: digit recognition on MNIST (LeCun et al., 1998), SVHN (Netzer et al., 2011) and object classification on FashionMNIST (Xiao et al., 2017), CIFAR10 (Krizhevsky et al., 2009). We test our method using six standard deep network architectures: MLP, ConvNet (Gidaris & Komodakis, 2018), LeNet (LeCun et al., 1998), AlexNet (Krizhevsky et al., 2012), VGG-11 (Simonyan & Zisserman, 2014) and ResNet-18 (He et al., 2016). MLP is a multilayer perceptron with two nonlinear hidden layers, each has 128 units. ConvNet is a commonly used modular architecture in few-shot

learning (Snell et al., 2017; Vinyals et al., 2016; Gidaris & Komodakis, 2018) with _D_ duplicate blocks, and each block has a convolutional layer with _W_ (3 _×_ 3) filters, a normalization layer _N_ , an activation layer _A_ and a pooling layer _P_ , denoted as [ _W, N, A, P_ ] _× D_ . The default ConvNet (unless specified otherwise) includes 3 blocks, each with 128 filters, followed by InstanceNorm (Ulyanov et al., 2016), ReLU and AvgPooling modules. The final block is followed by a linear classifier. We use Kaiming initialization (He et al., 2015) for network weights. The synthetic images can be initialized from Gaussian noise or randomly selected real training images. More details about the datasets, networks and hyper-parameters can be found in the supplementary.

The pipeline for dataset condensation has two stages: learning the condensed images (denoted as C) and training classifiers from scratch on them (denoted as T). Note that the model architectures used in two stages might be different. For the coreset baselines, the coreset is selected in the first stage. We investigate three settings: 1, 10 and 50 image/class learning, which means that the condensed set or coreset contains 1, 10 and 50 images per class respectively. Each method is run for 5 times, and 5 synthetic sets are generated in the first stage; each generated synthetic set is used to train 20 randomly initialized models in the second stage and evaluated on the test set, which amounts to evaluating 100 models in the second stage. In all experiments, we report the mean and standard deviation of these 100 testing results.

**Baselines.** We compare our method to four coreset baselines (Random, Herding, K-Center and Forgetting) and also to DD (Wang et al., 2018). In Random, the training samples are randomly selected as the coreset. Herding baseline, which selects closest samples to the cluster center, is based on (Welling, 2009) and used in (Rebuffi et al., 2017; Castro et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2019; Belouadah & Popescu, 2020). K-Center (Wolf, 2011; Sener & Savarese, 2018) picks multiple center points such that the largest distance between a data point and its nearest center is minimized. For Herding and K-Center, we use models trained on the whole dataset to extract features, compute _l_ 2 distance to centers. Forgetting method (Toneva et al., 2019) selects the training samples which are easy to forget during training. We do not compare to GSS-Greedy (Aljundi et al., 2019), because it is also a similarity based greedy algorithm like K-Center, but GSS-Greedy trains an online learning model to measure the similarity of samples, which is different from general image classification problem. More detailed comparisons can be found in the supplementary.

**Comparison to coreset methods.** We first compare our method to the coreset baselines on MNIST, FashionMNIST, SVHN and CIFAR10 in Table 1 using the default ConvNet in classification accuracy. Whole dataset indicates training on the whole original set which serves as an approximate upper-bound performance. First we observe that our method outperforms all the baselines significantly and achieves a comparable result (98 _._ 8%) in case of 50 images per class to the upper bound (99 _._ 6%) in MNIST which uses two orders of magnitude more training images per class (6000). We also obtain promising results in FashionMNIST, however, the gap between our method and upper bound is bigger in SVHN and CIFAR10 which contain more diverse images with varying foregrounds and backgrounds. We also observe that, (i) the random selection baseline is competitive to other coreset methods in 10 and 50 images per class and (ii) herding method is on average the best coreset technique. We visualize the condensed images produced by our method under 1 image/class setting in Figure 2. Interestingly they are interpretable and look like “prototypes” of each class.

**Comparison to DD (Wang et al., 2018).** Unlike the setting in Table 1, DD (Wang et al., 2018) reports results only for 10 images per class on MNIST and CIFAR10 over LeNet and AlexCifarNet (a customized AlexNet). We strictly follow the experimental setting in (Wang et al., 2018), use the same architectures and report our and their original results in Table 3 for a fair comparison. Our method achieves significantly better performance than DD on both benchmarks; obtains 5% higher accuracy with only 1 synthetic sample per class than DD with 10 samples per class. In addition, our method obtains consistent results over multiple runs with a standard deviation of only 0 _._ 6% on MNIST, while DD’s performance significantly vary over different runs (8 _._ 1%). Finally our method trains 2 times faster than DD and requires 50% less memory on CIFAR10 experiments. More detailed runtime and qualitative comparison can be found in the supplementary.

**Cross-architecture generalization.** Another key advantage of our method is that the condensed images learned using one architecture can be used to train another unseen one. Here we learn 1 condensed image per class for MNIST over a diverse set of networks including MLP, ConvNet (Gidaris & Komodakis, 2018), LeNet (LeCun et al., 1998), AlexNet (Krizhevsky et al., 2012), VGG-11 (Simonyan & Zisserman, 2014) and ResNet-18 (He et al., 2016) (see Table 2). Once the condensed sets are synthesized, we train every network on all the sets separately from scratch and evaluate their cross architecture performance in terms of classification accuracy on the MNIST test set. Table 2 shows that the condensed images, especially the ones that are trained with convolutional networks, perform well and are thus architecture generic. MLP generated images do not work well for training convolutional architectures which is possibly due to the mismatch between translation invariance properties of MLP and convolutional networks. Interestingly, MLP achieves better performance with convolutional network generated images than the MLP generated ones. The best results are obtained in most cases with ResNet generated images and ConvNet or ResNet as classifiers which is inline with their performances when trained on the original dataset.

**Number of condensed images.** We also study the test performance of a ConvNet trained on them for MNIST, FashionMNIST, SVHN and CIFAR10 for various number of condensed images per class in Figure 3 in absolute and relative terms – normalized by its upper-bound. Increasing the number of condensed images improves the accuracies in all benchmarks and further closes the gap with the upper-bound performance especially in MNIST and FashionMNIST, while the gap remains larger in SVHN and CIFAR10. In addition, our method outperforms the coreset method - Herding by a large margin in all cases.

**Activation, normalization & pooling.** We also study the effect of various activation (sigmoid, ReLU (Nair & Hinton, 2010; Zeiler et al., 2013), leaky ReLU (Maas et al., 2013)), pooling (max, average) and normalization functions (batch (Ioffe & Szegedy, 2015), group (Wu & He, 2018), layer (Ba et al., 2016), instance norm (Ulyanov et al., 2016)) and have the following observations: i) leaky ReLU over ReLU and average pooling over max pooling enable learning better condensed images, as they allow for denser gradient flow; ii) instance normalization obtains better classifica-

## 3.2 APPLICATIONS

**Continual Learning** First we apply our method to a continual-learning scenario (Rebuffi et al., 2017; Castro et al., 2018) where new tasks are learned incrementally and the goal is to preserve the performance on the old tasks while learning the new ones. We build our model on E2E method in (Castro et al., 2018) that uses a limited budget rehearsal memory (we consider 10 images/class here) to keep representative samples from the old tasks and knowledge distillation (KD) to regularize the network’s output w.r.t. to previous predictions. We replace its sample selection mechanism (herding) with ours such that a set of condensed images are generated and stored in the memory, keep the rest of the model same and evaluate this model on the task-incremental learning problem on the digit recognition datasets, SVHN (Netzer et al., 2011), MNIST (LeCun et al., 1998) and USPS (Hull, 1994) in the same order. MNIST and USPS images are reshaped to 32 _×_ 32 RGB images.

We compare our method to E2E (Castro et al., 2018), depicted as herding in Figure 4, with and without KD regularization. The experiment contains 3 incremental training stages (SVHN _→_ MNIST _→_ USPS) and testing accuracies are computed by averaging over the test sets of the previous and current tasks after each stage. The desired outcome is to obtain high mean classification accuracy at T3. The results indicate that the condensed images are more _data-efficient_ than the ones sampled by herding and thus our method outperforms E2E in both settings, while by a larger margin (2 _._ 3% at T3) when KD is not employed.

**Neural Architecture Search.** Here we explore the use of our method in a simple neural architecture search (NAS) experiment on CIFAR10 which typically requires expensive training of numerous architectures multiple times on the whole training set and picking the best performing ones on a validation set. Our goal is to verify that our condensed images can be used to efficiently train multiple networks to identify the best network. To this end, we construct the search space of 720 ConvNets as described in Section 3.1 by varying hyper-parameters _W_ , _N_ , _A_ , _P_ , _D_ over an uniform grid (see supplementary for more details), train them for 100 epochs on three small proxy datasets (10 images/class) that are obtained with Random sampling, Herding and our method. Note that we train the condensed images for once only with the default ConvNet architecture and use them to train all kinds of architectures. We also compare to early-stopping (Li & Talwalkar, 2020) in which the model is trained on whole training set but with the same number of training iterations as the one required for the small proxy datasets, in other words, for the same amount of computations.

Table 4 depicts i) the average test performance of the best selected model over 5 runs when trained on the whole dataset, ii) Spearman’s rank correlation coefficient between the validation accuracies obtained by training the selected top 10 models on the proxy dataset and whole dataset, iii) time for training 720 architectures on a NVIDIA GTX1080-Ti GPU, and iv) memory print of the training images. Our method achieves the highest testing performance (84.5%) and performance correlation (0.79), meanwhile significantly decreases the the searching time (from 8604.3 to 18.8 minutes) and storage space (from 5 _×_ 10[4] to 1 _×_ 10[2] images) compared to whole-dataset training. The competitive early-stopping baseline achieves on par performance for the best performing model with

ours, however, the rank correlation (0.42) of top 10 models is significantly lower than ours (0.79) which indicates unreliable correlation of performances between early-stopping and whole-dataset training. Furthermore, early-stopping needs 100 times as many training images as ours needs. Note that the training time for synthetic images is around 50 minutes (for _K_ = 500) which is one time off and negligible cost when training thousands even millions of candidate architectures in NAS.

## 4 CONCLUSION

In this paper, we propose a dataset condensation method that learns to synthesize a small set of informative images. We show that these images are significantly more data-efficient than the same number of original images and the ones produced by the previous method, and they are not architecture dependent, can be used to train different deep networks. Once trained, they can be used to lower the memory print of datasets and efficiently train numerous networks which are crucial in continual learning and neural architecture search respectively. For future work, we plan to explore the use of condensed images in more diverse and thus challenging datasets like ImageNet (Deng et al., 2009) that contain higher resolution images with larger variations in appearance and pose of objects, background.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Coarse (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper introduces a compelling and influential idea—dataset condensation via gradient matching—that has sparked follow-up work in data-efficient training. It demonstrates strong empirical gains over coreset methods and DD across multiple architectures and applications, with particularly clean results in MNIST and ablation studies on design choices. The method’s scalability advantage over bi-level optimization approaches is well-motivated and practically significant.

Overview issues:

- Gradient matching objective lacks theoretical justification for generalization: The core claim—that matching gradients at intermediate steps ensures comparable model convergence and generalization—relies on an unproven assumption that similar gradient directions imply similar solutions in parameter space. While the paper argues that locally smooth models should behave similarly near equivalent weights, it does not establish conditions under which gradient alignment over early training steps translates to test performance parity. This is especially concerning when transferring condensed data across architectures, where gradient geometry may differ significantly. A more rigorous connection between gradient similarity and generalization gap is needed, possibly via stability analysis or PAC-Bayes bounds, to support the method’s broad applicability claims.

- Condensed sets evaluated only on same or simpler architectures: The cross-architecture evaluation in Table 2 tests models of comparable or lower capacity than those used for condensation (e.g., training MLP on images condensed with ResNet), but does not evaluate whether synthetic data generated with small networks can effectively train much larger, more complex ones (e.g., condensing with ConvNet then training Vision Transformer). Given the paper’s claim of architecture independence, this omission weakens the evidence. Real-world use cases like NAS require exactly this kind of transfer. Without such experiments, the limits of transferability remain unclear, especially given known sensitivity of gradient-based synthesis to model inductive biases.

- Overstatement of performance on complex datasets: The paper highlights high accuracy on MNIST (98.8% with 50 images/class) as near-optimal, but downplays poor absolute performance on CIFAR10—only 53.9% with 50 images/class, nearly 30 points below full training. While Figure 3 shows relative gains over baselines, this masks the fact that condensed models remain far from usable in practice for challenging vision tasks. The conclusion suggests straightforward scalability to ImageNet, yet no evidence supports this leap. The work would benefit from acknowledging fundamental limits of extreme compression on semantically rich, high-entropy datasets, rather than implying uniform effectiveness across domains.

- Insufficient comparison to concurrent and prior synthesis methods: The paper positions itself against coreset selection and DD (2018), but omits comparisons to more relevant contemporaneous approaches like distribution matching (Zhao & Bilen, 2021) or trajectory matching (Kang et al., 2022), which directly challenge the sufficiency of single-step gradient alignment. Given that these methods were either concurrent or built directly on this work, their absence in discussion or experiments creates a misleading impression of novelty and superiority. A fair benchmark against distribution-based alternatives—especially on transferability and semantic fidelity—would strengthen the paper’s contribution narrative.

- Hyperparameter sensitivity and reproducibility concerns: Algorithm 1 depends on multiple fragile hyperparameters: the number of outer-loop initializations $K$, inner-loop steps $T$, and learning rates for both model and synthetic data updates. The paper reports $K=500$ and $T=100$ as defaults but provides no ablation on their impact. Given the stochasticity in initialization and batch sampling, it is unclear how stable the resulting synthetic sets are across runs. The low standard deviation reported (e.g., ±0.5%) may reflect insufficient sampling rather than robustness. Without sensitivity analysis or open-sourced code, independent reproduction and practical deployment are hindered.

- Lack of semantic validity analysis in condensed images: While Figure 2 shows interpretable 'prototypes', the paper does not assess whether these synthetic images capture meaningful intra-class variation or are merely averaging artifacts. On CIFAR10, for instance, a single synthetic image per class cannot represent diverse object poses, backgrounds, or scales. The method risks producing 'hallucinated' features that fit gradients but lack semantic coherence, especially under high compression. Including human evaluation, feature diversity metrics, or downstream task performance on fine-grained classification would help validate that condensation preserves meaningful information, not just gradient statistics.

- No ablation of gradient matching depth: The method assumes matching gradients over multiple inner-loop steps (T) is beneficial, but no experiment verifies how T affects final performance. A natural question is whether matching at initialization only (T=1) suffices, or if curriculum matching over T>1 steps meaningfully improves generalization. The paper should ablate T in Table 1—comparing T=1 vs T=100 for 10 images/class on CIFAR10—and report accuracy differences. Without this, the motivation for the full curriculum framework remains empirical rather than demonstrated.

- Missing transfer to larger architectures: The paper claims condensed data are architecture-independent but never tests training a larger network than used in condensation. For example, synthetic images generated with ConvNet should be used to train ResNet-50 or Vision Transformer on CIFAR10. Readers will wonder whether gradient matching at scale preserves signal for deeper models. This computation is standard in NAS and model scaling studies; omitting it weakens the generality claim. The authors should add one experiment where condensed CIFAR10 images (from ConvNet) train a ResNet-50 and report final accuracy.

- No analysis of label learning feasibility: The paper states labels are kept fixed during condensation due to joint optimization challenges, but claims the method learns 'synthetic samples and their labels' in the abstract. This is contradictory. A minimal check would be to compute the gradient of the matching loss with respect to a single label (e.g., flipping one class index) and report its magnitude versus image gradients. If label gradients are negligible, the claim of learning labels should be revised. Otherwise, the joint optimization problem needs discussion.

- Unverified assumption of initialization robustness: The method averages over random initializations P(θ₀) to improve generalization, but provides no evidence that performance is stable across different P(θ₀). A natural check is to repeat the MNIST 1 image/class experiment using Xavier instead of Kaiming initialization for condensation, then test on models using both schemes. If accuracy drops significantly, the assumption of initialization invariance fails. This test is necessary to support the core design choice in Equation 5.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Provide theoretical justification linking gradient matching to generalization performance, possibly via optimization stability or feature alignment arguments.

- Add experiments showing transfer from small to significantly larger or different architectures (e.g., condense with ConvNet, train ViT or DenseNet) to substantiate architecture-independence claims.

- Acknowledge performance limitations on complex datasets like CIFAR10 and temper extrapolations to ImageNet with discussion of scalability barriers.

- Include comparisons to concurrent distribution-matching and trajectory-matching methods to properly position the contribution.

- Conduct and report ablation studies on key hyperparameters (K, T, η_S) and release code to ensure reproducibility.

comments.title: Claim of learning labels contradicts method description
comments.quote: The synthetic data contains not only samples but also their labels $(s,y)$ that can be jointly learned by optimizing eq. (9) in theory. However, their joint optimization is challenging, as the content of the samples depend on their label and vice-versa. Thus in our experiments we learn to synthesize images for fixed labels, e.g. one synthetic image per class.
comments.explanation: The abstract and this section claim the method can 'learn' labels in theory, but the actual implementation fixes labels throughout training. No ablation or analysis supports the feasibility of label optimization, and the gradient of the matching loss with respect to labels is not discussed. This creates a misleading impression that label learning is a viable extension, when in practice it is abandoned due to instability. The claim should be revised to reflect that only image content is optimized, and label learning remains speculative without empirical validation.

comments.title: Unjustified substitution in gradient matching derivation
comments.quote:
Based on our observation ($D(\bm{\theta}_{t}^{\mathcal{S}},\bm{\theta}_{t}^{\mathcal{T}})\approx 0$), we simplify the formulation in eq. (7) by replacing $\bm{\theta}_{t}^{\mathcal{T}}$ with $\bm{\theta}_{t}^{\mathcal{S}}$ and use $\bm{\theta}$ to denote $\bm{\theta}^{\mathcal{S}}$ in the rest of the paper:

$\min_{\mathcal{S}}\mathrm{E}_{\bm{\theta}_{0}\sim P_{\bm{\theta}_{0}}}[\sum_{t=0}^{T-1}D(\nabla_{\bm{\theta}}\mathcal{L}^{\mathcal{S}}(\bm{\theta}_{t}),\nabla_{\bm{\theta}}\mathcal{L}^{\mathcal{T}}(\bm{\theta}_{t}))].$ (9)
comments.explanation: The substitution of $\bm{\theta}_t^{\mathcal{T}}$ with $\bm{\theta}_t^{\mathcal{S}}$ in the gradient evaluation of $\mathcal{L}^{\mathcal{T}}$ assumes that $\nabla\mathcal{L}^{\mathcal{T}}$ is stable under small parameter changes, but this requires a Lipschitz condition on the gradient field that is neither stated nor verified. Without such a bound, even if $\|\bm{\theta}_t^{\mathcal{S}} - \bm{\theta}_t^{\mathcal{T}}\|$ is small, the gradient difference could be large—especially near sharp minima. The derivation should include an explicit smoothness assumption or empirical validation of gradient stability along the synthetic training path.

comments.title: Gradient matching does not imply parameter proximity
comments.quote: In our preliminary experiments, we observe that $\bm{\theta}_{t+1}^{\mathcal{S}}$, which is parameterized with $\mathcal{S}$, can successfully track $\bm{\theta}_{t+1}^{\mathcal{T}}$ by updating $\mathcal{S}$ and minimizing $D(\bm{\theta}_{t}^{\mathcal{S}},\bm{\theta}_{t}^{\mathcal{T}})$ close to zero.
comments.explanation: The claim that minimizing gradient divergence leads to parameter tracking assumes a stable integration of gradient differences over time. However, even small persistent gradient mismatches can accumulate under iterative updates, especially with large learning rates or ill-conditioned landscapes. The paper provides no bound on parameter divergence in terms of cumulative gradient differences, nor does it analyze the stability of the synthetic optimization trajectory. A brief discussion or empirical plot showing $\|\bm{\theta}_t^{\mathcal{S}} - \bm{\theta}_t^{\mathcal{T}}\|$ over $t$ would help justify this key assumption.

comments.title: Incorrect attribution of bi-level optimization framework
comments.quote:
One approach, which is proposed in *(Wang et al., 2018)* and extended in *(Sucholutsky and Schonlau, 2019; Bohdal et al., 2020; Such et al., 2020)*, is to pose the parameters $\bm{\theta}^{\mathcal{S}}$ as a function of the synthetic data $\mathcal{S}$:

$\mathcal{S}^{*}=\operatorname*{arg\,min}_{\mathcal{S}}\mathcal{L}^{\mathcal{T}}(\bm{\theta}^{\mathcal{S}}(\mathcal{S}))\qquad\text{subject to}\qquad\bm{\theta}^{\mathcal{S}}(\mathcal{S})=\operatorname*{arg\,min}_{\bm{\theta}}\mathcal{L}^{\mathcal{S}}(\bm{\theta}).$ (3)
comments.explanation: The bi-level formulation in equation (3) is not proposed in Wang et al. (2018), which focuses on learning data augmentation policies, not dataset distillation via nested optimization. The correct attribution for this framework is to later work such as Wang et al. (ICLR 2020) or Lorraine et al. (2020). Sucholutsky and Schonlau (2019) also do not introduce this formulation but focus on soft-label distillation. Misattributing this foundational setup undermines scholarly accuracy and misrepresents the method's novelty. The citation should be corrected to reflect the actual source of the bi-level framework.

comments.title: Misleading claim of avoiding unrolling
comments.quote: This approximation has the key advantage over *(Wang et al., 2018)* and eq. (5) that it does not require the expensive unrolling of the recursive computation graph over the previous parameters $\{\bm{\theta}_{0},\ldots,\bm{\theta}_{t-1}\}$. The important consequence is that the optimization is significantly faster, memory efficient and thus scales up to the state-of-the-art deep neural networks (e.g. ResNet *(He et al., 2016)*).
comments.explanation: The method still requires backpropagation through the synthetic training trajectory $\{\bm{\theta}_t\}_{t=0}^{T-1}$, each of which depends on $\mathcal{S}$. This necessitates storing or recomputing intermediate states—equivalent to backpropagation through time—and incurs similar memory costs to unrolling in Wang et al. (2018). The claim of avoiding unrolling is therefore incorrect unless checkpointing or truncation is used, which is not mentioned. The advantage should be reframed as avoiding second-order derivatives or implicit gradients, not unrolling per se.

comments.title: Overstated claim of architecture independence
comments.quote: We show that these images are significantly more data-efficient than the same number of original images and the ones produced by the previous method, and they are not architecture dependent, can be used to train different deep networks.
comments.explanation: The claim of full architecture independence is too strong. The cross-architecture experiments in Table 2 only test transfer among models of similar or smaller capacity than the condensation model (e.g., ResNet to MLP), but do not evaluate whether synthetic data generated with a small network can train a much larger one (e.g., ConvNet → ResNet-50). Given the known sensitivity of gradient-based synthesis to model inductive biases and the performance drop on CIFAR10, the claim should be tempered to reflect that transfer works best within similar families or capacities, not universally across architectures.

comments.title: Inconsistent performance claim in DD comparison
comments.quote: Our method achieves significantly better performance than DD on both benchmarks; obtains  $5\%$  higher accuracy with only 1 synthetic sample per class than DD with 10 samples per class.
comments.explanation: The 5% improvement claim appears to conflate results from MNIST and CIFAR10. On CIFAR10 with 10 images/class, the method achieves 39.1±1.2%, while DD achieves 36.8±1.2%—a difference of 2.3%, not 5%. The 5% gap is closer to the MNIST result at 10 images/class (~4.4%). This exaggerates the margin on CIFAR10 and misleads readers about the consistency of gains. The sentence should report dataset-specific improvements separately.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated explanation for MLP-to-CNN transfer failure
comments.quote: MLP generated images do not work well for training convolutional architectures which is possibly due to the mismatch between translation invariance properties of MLP and convolutional networks.
comments.explanation: The explanation reverses the expected inductive bias: MLPs are not translation-invariant, while CNNs are translation-equivariant. The failure of MLP-generated images on CNNs is more likely due to differences in gradient structure or feature hierarchy during condensation, not translation properties. The reverse transfer—CNN-generated images working well on MLPs—suggests that spatially structured gradients benefit fully connected networks. The current explanation lacks mechanistic support and should be replaced with a more plausible hypothesis based on gradient alignment or frequency content.

comments.title: Syntactic error in gradient matching loss
comments.quote:
and

$d(\mathbf{A},\mathbf{B})=\sum_{i=1}^{\mathrm{out}}\left(1-\frac{\mathbf{A_{i}}\cdot\mathbf{B_{i}}}{\|\mathbf{A_{i}}\cdot\|\|\mathbf{B_{i}}\cdot\|}\right)$ (10)

where
comments.explanation: The denominator $\|\mathbf{A_{i}}\cdot\|\|\mathbf{B_{i}}\cdot\|$ is malformed—likely a typo for $\|\mathbf{A_{i}}\| \|\mathbf{B_{i}}\|$. The current form suggests a dot product followed by a norm, which is undefined. Additionally, the description of $\mathbf{A_i}$ as gradients 'corresponding to each output node' needs clarification: is it the gradient row for output $i$, or does it include input dimensions? Rewrite equation (10) with correct norms and explicitly define $\mathbf{A_i}$ as the flattened gradient vector for the $i$-th output neuron across all input connections.

comments.title: Ambiguity in $\bm{\theta}^{\mathcal{T}}$ dependence on initialization
comments.quote: where $\bm{\theta}^{\mathcal{T}}=\operatorname*{arg\,min}_{\bm{\theta}}\mathcal{L}^{\mathcal{T}}(\bm{\theta}(\bm{\theta}_{0}))$. For brevity, we use only $\bm{\theta}^{\mathcal{S}}$ and $\bm{\theta}^{\mathcal{T}}$ to indicate $\bm{\theta}^{\mathcal{S}}(\bm{\theta}_{0})$ and $\bm{\theta}^{\mathcal{T}}(\bm{\theta}_{0})$ respectively in the next sections.
comments.explanation: The notation $\bm{\theta}^{\mathcal{T}}(\bm{\theta}_0)$ suggests the minimizer depends on initialization, but $\operatorname*{arg\,min}_{\bm{\theta}} \mathcal{L}^{\mathcal{T}}(\bm{\theta})$ is a property of the loss landscape. In deep learning, multiple local minima mean different $\bm{\theta}_0$ can lead to different solutions. The expectation over $P_{\bm{\theta}_0}$ only makes sense if $\bm{\theta}^{\mathcal{T}}(\bm{\theta}_0)$ denotes the result of training from $\bm{\theta}_0$, not a fixed global minimizer. Clarify that $\bm{\theta}^{\mathcal{T}}(\bm{\theta}_0)$ is the outcome of optimization starting from $\bm{\theta}_0$, and confirm it is recomputed for each sample in the expectation.

comments.title: Misleading comparison to early-stopping baseline
comments.quote:
The competitive early-stopping baseline achieves on par performance for the best performing model with

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

ours, however, the rank correlation (0.42) of top 10 models is significantly lower than ours (0.79) which indicates unreliable correlation of performances between early-stopping and whole-dataset training. Furthermore, early-stopping needs 100 times as many training images as ours needs.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'on par performance' is ambiguous—it refers only to the final test accuracy of the selected model after full retraining, not the quality of the search process. Early-stopping uses the full dataset, making it incomparable in efficiency. Readers may interpret this as equivalent performance under similar conditions, which is false. Clarify that 'on par' applies only after full retraining, and emphasize that early-stopping does not offer memory or data efficiency.

comments.title: Overstated scalability to ImageNet
comments.quote: For future work, we plan to explore the use of condensed images in more diverse and thus challenging datasets like ImageNet *(Deng et al., 2009)* that contain higher resolution images with larger variations in appearance and pose of objects, background.
comments.explanation: The statement implies a straightforward path to ImageNet, but the method achieves only 53.9% on CIFAR10 with 50 images/class—nearly 30 points below full training. This suggests fundamental limits in preserving semantic information under extreme compression. Without addressing these gaps, projecting success to ImageNet overstates readiness. The future work should acknowledge current performance limitations before discussing scale-up.

comments.title: Incorrect claim about random baseline competitiveness
comments.quote: We also observe that, (i) the random selection baseline is competitive to other coreset methods in 10 and 50 images per class and (ii) herding method is on average the best coreset technique.
comments.explanation: The claim understates random selection's performance. On CIFAR10 with 50 images/class, random (43.4%) outperforms herding (40.4%), K-Center (27.0%), and Forgetting (23.3%). On MNIST at 10 and 50 images/class, random also beats herding. Thus, random is not merely 'competitive'—it often wins. Conversely, herding is not consistently best. The observation should be revised to reflect that random selection frequently outperforms structured coreset methods, challenging their assumed superiority.

comments.title: Misleading claim about per-class gradient matching cost
comments.quote: imitating the mean gradients w.r.t. the data from single class is easier compared to those of multiple classes. This does not bring any extra computational cost.
comments.explanation: The claim that per-class matching 'does not bring any extra computational cost' ignores potential overhead from sequential updates. While it reduces memory, processing classes one at a time may increase total wall-clock time compared to parallel full-batch updates. The statement should be revised to acknowledge that computational cost depends on implementation and hardware, and that 'easier' is an empirical observation, not a formal complexity advantage.

comments.title: Ambiguous use of 'upper-bound' in accuracy discussion
comments.quote: Increasing the number of condensed images improves the accuracies in all benchmarks and further closes the gap with the upper-bound performance especially in MNIST and FashionMNIST, while the gap remains larger in SVHN and CIFAR10.
comments.explanation: The term 'upper-bound' is not defined. It refers to the full-dataset performance in Table 1, but this should be stated explicitly. Otherwise, readers may interpret it as a theoretical limit. Clarify that 'upper-bound' means the mean accuracy achieved when training on the entire dataset, and cite the specific values from Table 1 to avoid ambiguity.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
This paper introduces Dataset Condensation with Gradient Matching, a method to synthesize small sets of informative samples that can replace large datasets for training neural networks. The core idea is to match gradients between models trained on synthetic versus real data. While the results show impressive performance on several benchmarks, there are significant concerns about the method's scalability, generalization claims, and the validity of gradient matching as a surrogate objective.

The paper presents a novel gradient matching approach for dataset condensation that achieves strong empirical results on standard benchmarks. The method appears technically sound and offers clear computational advantages over prior work like Dataset Distillation. The cross-architecture generalization results and applications to continual learning and NAS are particularly compelling. However, the paper's claims about generalization to complex datasets and architectures lack sufficient evidence, and the scalability limitations are not adequately addressed.

Overview issues:

- Scalability claims not validated for large-scale datasets: The paper claims the method scales to state-of-the-art networks like ResNet, but only demonstrates results on small datasets (MNIST, FashionMNIST, SVHN, CIFAR10). The conclusion mentions future work on ImageNet but provides no evidence or analysis of computational feasibility for high-resolution images. The gradient matching objective requires computing gradients over the entire real dataset at each iteration, which becomes prohibitive for large datasets. The paper should include computational complexity analysis and preliminary results on larger datasets to support scalability claims.

- Cross-architecture generalization insufficiently demonstrated: While Table 2 shows cross-architecture results for MNIST, these are limited to simple digit recognition tasks. The paper's claim that condensed images work across different architectures is not validated on more complex datasets like CIFAR10 or ImageNet. The literature context notes that synthetic data performance varies with network changes, yet the paper only tests this on MNIST where the task is relatively simple. Cross-architecture evaluation on at least one complex dataset is needed to substantiate this key claim.

- Gradient matching validity as optimization surrogate not justified: The paper assumes that matching gradients at each iteration ensures similar final model performance, but provides no theoretical justification for this approximation. The leap from parameter matching (Eq. 4) to gradient matching (Eq. 9) is presented as an empirical observation without analysis of when this approximation might fail. Given that gradients can be similar while leading to different optima in non-convex landscapes, the paper should analyze the conditions under which gradient matching preserves the optimization trajectory and final solution quality.

- Mode collapse and diversity preservation not analyzed: The method learns one synthetic image per class by default, which risks collapsing intra-class diversity. While Figure 2 shows interpretable 'prototype' images, the paper does not analyze whether this leads to mode collapse or loss of important variations within classes. The literature context highlights this as a known limitation of gradient-based methods. The paper should evaluate diversity preservation, perhaps by measuring feature space coverage or testing on out-of-distribution samples that require intra-class variation.

- Computational overhead of condensation process not quantified: The paper emphasizes computational benefits during training on condensed data but does not quantify the cost of the condensation process itself. Algorithm 1 shows K outer loops and T inner loops with gradient computations over the full dataset, yet no timing analysis is provided. The comparison to DD mentions 2x faster training but not the total condensation time. For practical adoption, readers need to understand the full computational budget including both condensation and subsequent training phases.

- Missing demonstration of gradient matching failure cases: The paper presents gradient matching as a surrogate for parameter matching but never shows when this approximation breaks down. A theory paper should include at least one concrete example where similar gradients lead to different final parameters or poor performance. For instance, construct a simple 2-layer network with specific weight initialization where gradient matching succeeds in early iterations but diverges later, demonstrating the limits of the approximation. This would help readers understand the method's boundaries and when they might need to use the more expensive parameter matching approach.

- No worked computation of gradient matching loss: Despite introducing the gradient matching loss in equation (10), the paper never walks through a concrete computation. Readers would benefit from seeing the loss calculated for a simple network like a 2-layer MLP on synthetic data. Show how the gradients are computed, flattened, and how the cosine similarity is calculated for each output node. This worked example would clarify implementation details and help verify that the layer-wise grouping approach actually works as claimed.

- Cross-architecture generalization not tested on complex data: The paper's claim that condensed images work across architectures is only validated on MNIST in Table 2. For a meaningful demonstration, the authors should show cross-architecture results on at least one complex dataset like CIFAR10. For example, learn condensed images using ResNet-18 on CIFAR10, then train VGG-11 and AlexNet on those same images and report performance. Without this, the architecture-independence claim appears limited to simple digit recognition tasks.

- Missing analysis of intra-class diversity preservation: The method learns one synthetic image per class by default, but the paper doesn't analyze whether this captures intra-class variation. The authors should measure diversity preservation, perhaps by training a classifier on condensed data and testing on out-of-distribution samples that require understanding intra-class variation. Or compute feature space coverage of condensed vs original data using t-SNE visualizations. This analysis is crucial since the literature context highlights mode collapse as a potential issue for gradient-based methods.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Add experiments on at least one larger dataset (e.g., Tiny ImageNet) with computational analysis to support scalability claims

- Include cross-architecture generalization experiments on CIFAR10 or a more complex dataset beyond MNIST

- Provide theoretical or empirical analysis of when gradient matching might fail as a surrogate for parameter matching

- Analyze and report on intra-class diversity preservation in the condensed sets

- Report complete computational costs including condensation time and memory requirements

comments.title: Invalid gradient substitution in equation (9)
comments.quote:
Based on our observation ($D(\bm{\theta}_{t}^{\mathcal{S}},\bm{\theta}_{t}^{\mathcal{T}})\approx 0$), we simplify the formulation in eq. (7) by replacing $\bm{\theta}_{t}^{\mathcal{T}}$ with $\bm{\theta}_{t}^{\mathcal{S}}$ and use $\bm{\theta}$ to denote $\bm{\theta}^{\mathcal{S}}$ in the rest of the paper:

$\min_{\mathcal{S}}\mathrm{E}_{\mathbf{\theta}_{0}\sim P_{\mathbf{\theta}_{0}}}[\sum_{t=0}^{T-1}D(\nabla_{\mathbf{\theta}}\mathcal{L}^{\mathcal{S}}(\mathbf{\theta}_{t}),\nabla_{\mathbf{\theta}}\mathcal{L}^{\mathcal{T}}(\mathbf{\theta}_{t}))].$ (9)
comments.explanation: The derivation from equation (7) to (9) contains a logical gap. Equation (7) minimizes parameter distance $D(\bm{\theta}_{t}^{\mathcal{S}},\bm{\theta}_{t}^{\mathcal{T}})$, while equation (9) replaces this with gradient distance. The observation that parameters are close does not justify changing the objective function. From equation (8), if $\bm{\theta}_{t}^{\mathcal{S}} \approx \bm{\theta}_{t}^{\mathcal{T}}$, then matching gradients becomes a consequence rather than an objective. The paper should explicitly state that minimizing gradient distance serves as a proxy for parameter matching, or provide theoretical justification for this equivalence under specific conditions.

comments.title: Missing label in generalization expectation
comments.quote: The generalization performance of the obtained model $\phi_{\bm{\theta}^{\mathcal{T}}}$ can be written as $\mathbb{E}_{\bm{x}\sim P_{\mathcal{D}}}[\ell(\phi_{\bm{\theta}^{\mathcal{T}}}(\bm{x}),y)]$ where $P_{\mathcal{D}}$ is the data distribution.
comments.explanation: The expectation notation is mathematically incorrect. The expectation should be over the joint distribution of both inputs and labels, not just inputs. As written, $y$ is undefined in the expectation since it's not being sampled from $P_{\mathcal{D}}$. This should be $\mathbb{E}_{(\bm{x},y)\sim P_{\mathcal{D}}}[\ell(\phi_{\bm{\theta}^{\mathcal{T}}}(\bm{x}),y)]$ or specify that $y$ is the true label corresponding to $\bm{x}$. The same error appears in the subsequent comparison with $\phi_{\bm{\theta}^{\mathcal{S}}}$.

comments.title: Algorithm 1 missing gradient computation step
comments.quote: Update $\mathcal{S}_{c}\leftarrow$ opt-alg_{S}($D(\nabla_{\bm{\theta}}\mathcal{L}_{c}^{\mathcal{S}}(\bm{\theta}_{t}),\nabla_{\bm{\theta}}\mathcal{L}_{c}^{\mathcal{T}}(\bm{\theta}_{t})),\varsigma_{\mathcal{S}},\eta_{\mathcal{S}}$)
comments.explanation:
The algorithm shows updating synthetic samples using the gradient matching loss, but never explicitly shows the gradient computation step. Line 7 computes the losses, but there's no step showing that gradients $
abla_{ m{	heta}}\mathcal{L}_{c}^{\mathcal{S}}( m{	heta}_{t})$ and $
abla_{ m{	heta}}\mathcal{L}_{c}^{\mathcal{T}}( m{	heta}_{t})$ are actually computed before being used in line 8. This is a critical missing step since the entire method relies on gradient matching. Add a line between 7 and 8: 'Compute gradients $
abla_{ m{	heta}}\mathcal{L}_{c}^{\mathcal{S}}( m{	heta}_{t})$ and $
abla_{ m{	heta}}\mathcal{L}_{c}^{\mathcal{T}}( m{	heta}_{t})$' to make the algorithm complete.

comments.title: Inconsistent constraint notation in equation (5)
comments.quote: $\min_{\mathcal{S}}\operatorname{E}_{\bm{\theta}_{0}\sim P_{\bm{\theta}_{0}}}[D(\bm{\theta}^{\mathcal{S}}(\bm{\theta}_{0}),\bm{\theta}^{\mathcal{T}}(\bm{\theta}_{0}))]\quad\text{subject to}\quad\bm{\theta}^{\mathcal{S}}(\mathcal{S})=\operatorname*{arg\,min}_{\bm{\theta}}\mathcal{L}^{\mathcal{S}}(\bm{\theta}(\bm{\theta}_{0}))$ (5)
comments.explanation: There's a notation inconsistency in equation (5). The objective function shows $\bm{\theta}^{\mathcal{S}}$ as a function of $\bm{\theta}_{0}$, but the constraint uses $\bm{\theta}^{\mathcal{S}}(\mathcal{S})$ on the left side, suggesting dependence only on $\mathcal{S}$. The right side $\mathcal{L}^{\mathcal{S}}(\bm{\theta}(\bm{\theta}_{0}))$ is also problematic since the inner optimization should be over $\bm{\theta}$ given fixed $\mathcal{S}$ and $\bm{\theta}_{0}$. The constraint should consistently show $\bm{\theta}^{\mathcal{S}}$ depends on both $\mathcal{S}$ and $\bm{\theta}_{0}$.

comments.title: Undefined distance function D in optimization
comments.quote: $\min_{\mathcal{S}}D(\bm{\theta}^{\mathcal{S}},\bm{\theta}^{\mathcal{T}})\quad\text{subject to}\quad\bm{\theta}^{\mathcal{S}}(\mathcal{S})=\operatorname*{arg\,min}_{\bm{\theta}}\mathcal{L}^{\mathcal{S}}(\bm{\theta})$ (4)
comments.explanation: The paper introduces the distance function $D(\cdot,\cdot)$ in equation (4) without defining its specific form or properties. This is problematic because the choice of distance function significantly affects the optimization landscape. For neural network parameters, common choices like Euclidean distance may not capture functional equivalence due to permutation symmetries and scale invariances. The paper should specify whether $D$ is Euclidean distance, or a more sophisticated metric that accounts for these symmetries.

comments.title: Unjustified smoothness assumption implication
comments.quote: Let $\phi_{\bm{\theta}}$ be a locally smooth function, similar weights ($\bm{\theta}^{\mathcal{S}}\approx\bm{\theta}^{\mathcal{T}}$) imply similar mappings in a local neighborhood and thus generalization performance, *i.e*. $\mathbb{E}_{\bm{x}\sim P_{\mathcal{D}}}[\ell(\phi_{\bm{\theta}^{\mathcal{T}}}(\bm{x}),y)]\simeq\mathbb{E}_{\bm{x}\sim P_{\mathcal{D}}}[\ell(\phi_{\bm{\theta}^{\mathcal{S}}}(\bm{x}),y)]$.
comments.explanation: The claim that local smoothness of $\phi_{\bm{\theta}}$ implies similar generalization performance from similar weights is not justified. Local smoothness only guarantees that outputs are similar for similar inputs given fixed parameters, not that similar parameters produce similar generalization performance across the entire data distribution. The implication from parameter similarity to generalization equivalence requires additional assumptions about the loss landscape and the relationship between parameter distance and functional difference.

comments.title: Algorithm 1 inconsistent batch usage
comments.quote: Update $\bm{\theta}_{t+1}\leftarrow$ opt-alg_{θ}($\mathcal{L}^{\mathcal{S}}(\bm{\theta}_{t}),\varsigma_{\bm{\theta}},\eta_{\bm{\theta}}$) $\triangleright$ Use the whole $\mathcal{S}$
comments.explanation: The algorithm has an inconsistency in batch usage. Lines 6-7 process minibatches $B_{c}^{\mathcal{T}}$ and $B_{c}^{\mathcal{S}}$ per class, but line 9 suddenly switches to using 'the whole $\mathcal{S}$' for updating $\bm{\theta}$. This is confusing because the algorithm was designed to process class-wise minibatches for memory efficiency, but then uses all synthetic data for the weight update. Either clarify that this is intentional (and explain why) or make it consistent by using class-wise minibatches for the weight update as well.

comments.title: Missing justification for class-wise processing
comments.quote: Note that we sample each real and synthetic batch pair from $\mathcal{T}$ and $\mathcal{S}$ containing samples from a single class and the synthetic data for each class are separately (or parallelly) updated at each iteration ($t$) for the following reasons: i) this reduces memory use at train time, ii) imitating the mean gradients w.r.t. the data from single class is easier compared to those of multiple classes. This does not bring any extra computational cost.
comments.explanation: The claim that class-wise processing 'does not bring any extra computational cost' needs justification. Processing classes separately requires C separate gradient computations and updates per iteration, whereas processing all classes together would require only one gradient computation over the full batch. The authors should provide a computational complexity analysis showing that the total operations are equivalent, or clarify what they mean by 'extra computational cost'.

comments.title: Architecture independence claim overstated
comments.quote: and they are not architecture dependent, can be used to train different deep networks.
comments.explanation: The claim that condensed images are 'not architecture dependent' overstates what the paper actually demonstrates. According to the paper's own results, cross-architecture generalization was only tested on MNIST (Table 2), which is a relatively simple digit recognition task. The paper did not validate this claim on more complex datasets like CIFAR10 or SVHN where architecture dependence might be more pronounced. The formal results only establish architecture independence for the specific case of MNIST, not as a general property of the method.

comments.title: Data efficiency claim lacks qualification
comments.quote: We show that these images are significantly more data-efficient than the same number of original images and the ones produced by the previous method
comments.explanation: This statement needs qualification because the paper's own results show that the performance gap varies significantly across datasets. While the method achieves 98.8% vs 99.6% on MNIST (a small gap), the gap is 'bigger in SVHN and CIFAR10 which contain more diverse images.' The claim of 'significantly more data-efficient' is not uniformly supported across all tested datasets, particularly the more challenging ones where the method's advantage is less clear.

comments.title: Non-standard notation for condensed set
comments.quote: Our goal is to generate a small set of condensed synthetic samples with their labels, $\mathcal{S}=\{(\bm{s}_{i},y_{i})\}|_{i=1}^{|\mathcal{S}|}$ where $\bm{s}\in\mathbb{R}^{d}$ and $y\in\mathcal{Y}$, $|\mathcal{S}|\ll|\mathcal{T}|$.
comments.explanation: The notation $\{(\bm{s}_{i},y_{i})\}|_{i=1}^{|\mathcal{S}|}$ is non-standard and inconsistent with the notation used for $\mathcal{T}$ earlier in the text. For consistency with $\mathcal{T} = \{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{|\mathcal{T}|}$, this should be written as $\mathcal{S} = \{(\bm{s}_{i},y_{i})\}_{i=1}^{|\mathcal{S}|}$. The vertical bar notation $\{\cdot\}|_{i=1}^{n}$ is not standard mathematical notation for indexed sets.

comments.title: Equation (10) dimension mismatch
comments.quote:
$d(\mathbf{A},\mathbf{B})=\sum_{i=1}^{\mathrm{out}}\left(1-\frac{\mathbf{A_{i}}\cdot\mathbf{B_{i}}}{\|\mathbf{A_{i}}\|\|\mathbf{B_{i}}\|}\right)$ (10)

where $\mathbf{A_{i}}$ and $\mathbf{B_{i}}$ are flattened vectors of gradients corresponding to each output node $i$, which is in dimensional for FC weights and in$\times$h$\times$w dimensional for convolutional weights.
comments.explanation: There's a dimension mismatch in the description of $\mathbf{A_{i}}$ and $\mathbf{B_{i}}$. For FC layers, the text says the gradients are 'in dimensional' but this is incomplete - it should specify that for FC weights, the gradient tensor has dimensions out×in, so when flattened per output node, each $\mathbf{A_{i}}$ and $\mathbf{B_{i}}$ should be in-dimensional vectors. The current text is garbled and unclear.

comments.title: Inconsistent ratio calculation in Table 1
comments.explanation: The column header says 'Ratio (%)' but the values appear to be in percentage points (0.02% rather than 0.0002). This creates confusion about whether these are percentages or decimal fractions. Rewrite the column header as 'Ratio' and remove the % symbol, or clarify that these values represent actual percentages.

comments.title: Inconsistent upper bound values between tables
comments.explanation: There is an inconsistency in the reported upper bound performance for MNIST between Table 1 (99.6±0.0) and Table 3 (99.5±0.0). While this difference is small (0.1%), it raises questions about experimental consistency. The paper should clarify whether these represent different experimental runs, different architectures, or if this is a reporting error.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
The paper introduces a dataset condensation technique that synthesizes a small set of training images by matching the gradients of a network trained on synthetic data to those of a network trained on the full original dataset. The proposed method aims to reduce the computational cost of training models on large-scale datasets.

The paper provides a clever and computationally efficient alternative to existing dataset distillation methods by focusing on gradient matching rather than unrolling recursive optimization paths. The empirical results on standard benchmarks are strong and demonstrate significant gains in data efficiency and training speed.

Overview issues:

- Unclear Generalization Guarantees Across Architectures: While Section 3.1 demonstrates that condensed images can be used to train different architectures, the paper lacks a theoretical or empirical explanation for why gradient matching at the weight level should transfer well across models with different inductive biases. The results in Table 2 show performance variance, yet there is no analysis of whether the synthetic images are overfitting to the specific architectural features (e.g., filter sizes or normalization layers) of the teacher model used during the condensation process. To address this, the authors should provide an ablation study comparing the performance of synthetic sets generated by different architectures (e.g., ResNet vs. MLP) when tested on a wider range of unseen architectures, specifically analyzing the feature representation similarity.

- Sensitivity to Weight Initialization and Optimization Stability: Equation 9 defines the objective as an expectation over weight initializations, but the practical implementation in Algorithm 1 relies on stochastic sampling of these initializations. The paper does not analyze the variance of the resulting synthetic set quality relative to the distribution of initializations $P_{\theta_0}$. If the condensation process is highly sensitive to the specific random seeds used during the outer loop, the reproducibility of the synthetic sets is compromised. The authors should include a sensitivity analysis showing how the final test performance fluctuates based on the number of outer-loop iterations and the diversity of the initial weight samples.

- Lack of Discussion on Long-term Training Stability: The method relies on matching gradients at each step $t$ in the curriculum (Section 2.3). However, it is unclear if the synthetic dataset remains effective when the model is trained for a significantly larger number of epochs than the $\varsigma_{\theta}$ steps used during the condensation phase. There is a risk that the synthetic images provide a 'shortcut' that works for short-duration training but fails to converge to the global optimum of the original dataset. A more thorough evaluation of the training dynamics, perhaps by plotting the loss curves of models trained on synthetic versus real data over extended training durations, would clarify whether this method is a true replacement for full-dataset training or merely an initialization heuristic.

- Absence of Robustness Analysis Against Distribution Shift: The paper focuses on standard i.i.d. benchmarks like CIFAR-10 and MNIST. Given that the synthetic images are optimized to match gradients on a fixed training set, their behavior under distribution shift—where the test set might contain samples with different statistics—is unknown. It is possible that the gradient matching objective causes the synthetic set to collapse into a representation that is overly specific to the training distribution. Including an experiment where the synthetic set is evaluated on a slightly perturbed or out-of-distribution test set would significantly strengthen the claims regarding the general utility of the condensed data.

- Lack of empirical validation for gradient matching convergence: The paper assumes that matching gradients at each step $t$ ensures that the synthetic model follows a similar trajectory to the full-dataset model. However, it never explicitly validates this claim by plotting the trajectory of the parameters $\theta_t^{\mathcal{S}}$ and $\theta_t^{\mathcal{T}}$ in the parameter space, nor does it quantify the evolution of the distance $D(\theta_t^{\mathcal{S}}, \theta_t^{\mathcal{T}})$ over the course of training. To demonstrate that the result has bite, the authors should provide a visualization of the parameter trajectories for a simple model (e.g., a small MLP on MNIST) to confirm that the curriculum gradient matching actually forces the synthetic-trained model to follow the path of the full-data model, rather than just arriving at a similar final performance by chance.

- Missing computational complexity analysis for the condensation process: The paper claims the method is faster and more memory-efficient than Dataset Distillation, yet it lacks a concrete comparison of the computational cost of the condensation phase itself. While the runtime for training downstream architectures is reported, the time and memory required to compute the gradient matching objective for various dataset sizes remain opaque. The paper should include a table comparing the wall-clock time and peak GPU memory usage of the condensation phase for the reported benchmarks, specifically contrasting the proposed gradient matching with the unrolling-based approach of the original Dataset Distillation (Wang et al., 2018) to justify the claim of scalability.

Recommendation: minor revision. The paper presents a compelling and well-motivated method that outperforms existing baselines, but it requires a more rigorous investigation into the limitations of its cross-architecture generalization and the stability of the synthetic data under varying training regimes.

Revision targets:

- Include a sensitivity analysis of the synthetic set quality relative to the number of outer-loop gradient matching iterations and weight initialization diversity.

- Provide an ablation study comparing the cross-architecture transfer performance using synthetic sets generated by models with different capacities (e.g., shallow vs. deep).

- Add a discussion or empirical analysis regarding the training stability and convergence behavior of models trained on synthetic sets over extended training epochs.

- Include a brief analysis of the synthetic image quality under slight distribution shifts to address potential overfitting to the training set.

comments.title: Unjustified claim of architecture independence
comments.quote: We show that these images are significantly more data-efficient than the same number of original images and the ones produced by the previous method, and they are not architecture dependent, can be used to train different deep networks.
comments.explanation: The claim that synthetic images are 'not architecture dependent' is too strong. The performance variance in Table 2 demonstrates that the effectiveness of the condensed set is influenced by the architecture used during the condensation process. Please rephrase this to state that the images exhibit cross-architecture transferability, as the current wording implies an independence that the empirical results do not support.

comments.title: Inconsistency in NAS time comparison
comments.quote: Our method achieves the highest testing performance (84.5%) and performance correlation (0.79), meanwhile significantly decreases the the searching time (from 8604.3 to 18.8 minutes)
comments.explanation: The reported search time of 18.8 minutes excludes the 50-minute condensation phase. Comparing this to the 8604.3 minutes required for full-dataset training is misleading, as the latter does not require a separate condensation step. Please clarify the total time cost, including the condensation phase, to provide a fair comparison.

comments.title: Inconsistent reporting of MNIST upper-bound
comments.quote: achieves a comparable result (98.8%) in case of 50 images per class to the upper bound (99.6%) in MNIST
comments.explanation: The text cites the MNIST upper-bound as 99.6%, while Table 3 reports it as 99.5%. Please verify the correct value and ensure it is reported consistently throughout the paper.

coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
The paper introduces a dataset condensation method that matches gradients between networks trained on original and synthetic data. It presents empirical results on standard vision benchmarks and demonstrates applications in continual learning and neural architecture search. The approach offers a computationally lighter alternative to nested optimization methods by tracking gradient trajectories throughout training. However, the derivation of the core objective lacks algorithmic detail, and the evaluation omits critical scalability and robustness analyses.

The paper tackles a relevant problem in data-efficient learning and proposes a practical gradient-matching formulation that outperforms coreset baselines on small datasets. The cross-architecture experiments and NAS application show promise for reducing computational overhead in downstream tasks. The empirical results are generally strong for the tested benchmarks, but the methodological transparency and evaluation rigor need strengthening before the claims can be fully trusted.

Overview issues:

- Hand-wavy derivation of the gradient matching objective: The transition from the nested optimization in Eq. (7) to the simplified gradient matching loss in Eq. (9) relies on the assumption that $D(\bm{\theta}_t^{\mathcal{S}},\bm{\theta}_t^{\mathcal{T}})\approx 0$. This leap bypasses the actual computation of $\nabla_{\mathcal{S}} D$, which is the core of the method. Algorithm 1 updates $\mathcal{S}$ using `opt-alg_S` but never specifies how the gradient of the matching loss with respect to the synthetic images is obtained. Does it use truncated unrolling, implicit differentiation, or a first-order approximation? Without clarifying the backpropagation path through the inner optimization loop, the claim that the method avoids "expensive unrolling" is unsupported. The authors should explicitly detail the gradient computation strategy and provide a computational complexity analysis to substantiate the speed claims.

- Overfitting to specific training dynamics and lack of robustness analysis: The condensed dataset is optimized to match gradients produced by a specific optimizer, learning rate, batch size, and architecture. The paper claims the method yields architecture-generic images, but Table 2 reveals high variance and significant performance drops when cross-architecture transfer occurs. The method does not address how sensitive the condensed set is to changes in training protocols, such as different optimizers or data augmentation pipelines. If the synthetic data is tightly coupled to the reference training dynamics, its utility for general downstream tasks is limited. The authors should include an ablation study testing the robustness of the condensed dataset under varied training protocols and discuss the conditions under which gradient matching fails to generalize.

- Insufficient evaluation of computational scalability: While the paper claims the approach scales to state-of-the-art deep neural networks, the experimental section only reports results on relatively small datasets and modest architectures. The literature context correctly flags that nested gradient computations remain a bottleneck for larger models. The paper provides a training time of "around 50 minutes" for NAS but omits the total wall-clock time and memory footprint for generating the condensed datasets on CIFAR-10. Without quantifying the computational cost relative to the baseline methods or the full training process, the scalability claim remains unverified. I recommend adding a detailed runtime and memory analysis for the condensation phase across different dataset sizes and network depths, and explicitly comparing the cost to training a model from scratch on the original data.

- Questionable distance metric and lack of theoretical justification: The matching loss $D$ in Eq. (10) computes cosine similarity per output node across layers, claiming it outperforms flattened vector distances. The paper only references the supplementary for justification and does not explain why grouping by output node preserves semantic information better. Cosine similarity ignores gradient magnitudes, which carry crucial information about the loss landscape curvature and learning progress. Matching only the direction of gradients could lead to trivial solutions where the synthetic data produces gradients that point in the same direction but with wildly different scales. The authors should provide a theoretical or empirical justification for the per-output-node cosine similarity, analyze the impact of gradient magnitude on the condensed dataset quality, and include an ablation comparing $D$ to L2 or magnitude-aware metrics.

- Incomplete and inconsistent evaluation protocols: The experimental evaluation lacks statistical rigor and consistency across tasks. Table 1 reports mean and standard deviation over 5 runs with 100 models each, but Figure 4 and Figure 3 show single deterministic curves without error bars or confidence intervals. The NAS experiment reports Spearman correlation over 5 runs, yet the continual learning setup does not specify how many random seeds or model initializations were averaged. Furthermore, the comparison to Dataset Distillation only tests 10 images/class on specific architectures, while the main results use 1, 10, and 50 images/class. This inconsistency makes it difficult to assess whether the performance gains are due to the method itself or variations in the condensation ratio. The authors should standardize the evaluation protocol, report statistical uncertainty for all figures, and ensure fair comparisons across all condensation ratios and architectures.

- Risk of synthetic data artifacts and limited diversity preservation: The paper acknowledges that the goal is to generate informative samples rather than realistic ones, but Figure 2 shows condensed images that appear as noisy prototypes. Gradient matching alone does not enforce diversity or prevent mode collapse, especially when matching a large dataset to a single image per class. The method relies entirely on the gradient signal to guide the synthetic images, which can easily overfit to the specific classes or initialization patterns of the reference model. Without explicit regularization, the condensed set may fail to capture the full semantic variety of the original data. The authors should discuss the limitations of gradient-only matching, analyze the diversity of the generated samples using feature-space metrics, and consider how the method performs on datasets with higher intra-class variance.

- Lack of toy model analysis to ground gradient matching intuition: The paper introduces a complex nested optimization objective but never tests it on a tractable, fully-specified model. Readers cannot verify whether the gradient matching principle actually aligns the parameter trajectories in a controlled setting. A natural next step is to compute the gradient matching objective for a simple linear regression or a 2-layer MLP on a synthetic 1D or 2D dataset.
